stories.title: The Smuggler's Compass
stories.text: Captain Vess of Verdant Strand had hidden the compass for three years—bone-carved, singing faintly at high tide, pulled from a drowned archive before the Dominion could seize it. The stars had gone dark for three heartbeats on 2026-07-01, and Vess knew why: Zareth the Astronomer-Prince had chosen to stop renewing them. The cosmos was winding down. She sailed from Greyport with a crew of six and a hold full of pre-Remaking silk—the only trade good the Wailing Scar merchants still craved. But at Distant Reef, three days northwest, she found her old partner Morra waiting with Dominion auditors. Morra's shadow had been sold. The auditors were hunting consciousness-fragments, and they'd paid her in crystal vials to guide them to Vess's cargo. Vess laughed—a sound like breaking glass. She offered the auditors a choice: take the silk and sail, or watch the compass sing the boundary-waters awake. The Tear was failing anyway. One bone-compass, properly played, might accelerate it enough to swallow a revenue cutter. The auditors took the silk. Morra wept as they departed.

stories.title: The Stonewarden's Vigil
stories.text: Kelith was sixteen when the Council of Thane-Lords named her Stonewarden of the Borderlands—guardian of the pre-human boundary, inheritor of a post her grandfather had held for forty winters. The boundary-stones sang in frequencies no human ear was meant to hear, but Kelith's blood was half-fae: her mother had bargained a century ago, and the gift passed down. On 2026-07-10, the stones sang differently. The melody fractured. Kelith climbed the Pale Crossing ridge and found the eastern seals cracking—not from the abyss side, but from within the pre-human wastes. Something was waking. She sent word to Pelimor, but she knew what they would do: send bureaucrats, form committees, debate whether the wastes were truly rising or merely flickering. The Remaking had made all authority uncertain. No one trusted anyone's ledgers anymore. Kelith took her grandmother's staff—meteoric iron wrapped in sinew and singing-bone—and walked the boundary alone. For three days she carved new seals, speaking in the old tongue her mother had taught her: words that tasted like starlight and rust.

stories.title: The Glass-Breaker's Mercy
stories.text: Niel Coldwater had forged weapon-glass for the Dominion for twelve years—consciousness-fragments melted into steel-bright blades that sang with stolen memory. On 2026-07-10, a junior auditor brought him a commission that made him set down his tools. A mother's plea: her daughter was imprisoned in Keshtir's Quarantine-House, her consciousness fractured across seven vessels. The auditors refused to reassemble her—fragments were worth more as currency than as a person. The mother offered everything: pre-Remaking silk, starlight-glass, even a wyvern's promise of safe passage to the Counter-Archive. Niel's hands remembered how to refuse. He told the auditor he'd need a week. Instead, he walked to the Lampwick Quarter and found Mestra the vault-keeper in her hidden workshop. Together, they designed a cipher-lock carved from bone and meteoric iron—keyed not to auditor ledgers but to the daughter's own voice. The lock they placed in the mother's hands along with a map to a waystation north of Pelimor, where bone-singers still worked in secret. "The lock," Niel told her, "will open any Dominion vessel when you speak her name.

stories.title: The Wyvern's Census
stories.text:
Kressa the Fragment-Hoarder had counted her gold for three centuries. On 2026-07-10, on the 4,782nd day since she'd begun the tally, she realized the counting no longer mattered. The Remaking had transformed every coin in her Ironspine cavern into worthless dust. She should have been enraged. Instead, she felt oddly liberated. A refugee child had wandered into her outer lair, fleeing Dominion hunters. Rather than incinerate the trespasser, Kressa had done something unprecedented: she had offered shelter. The child—a bone-singer apprentice named Ash—had begun cataloging the hoard not by weight or value, but by story. This one from a trade war. This from a merchant's retirement. They are not gold anymore. They are memory."

Kressa began to listen. Over weeks, she and Ash sorted the three-century hoard into new categories: coins of joy, coins of sorrow, coins of cleverness, coins of betrayal. The gold became an archive of human choice. When the Wyvern-Council sent emissaries demanding Kressa rejoin their hoarding, she refused. She had found something better than accumulation: witness.

stories.title: The Merchant's Third Choice
stories.text: Torvin the Spice-Merchant had walked the Wailing Scar for six months—that impossible market existing in three timelines at once, where pre-Remaking goods traded for consciousness-fragments at prices that shifted hourly. On 2026-07-10, the Scar began to collapse. The three versions of reality that had overlapped there were diverging. Torvin could see it: the same stall-keeper appearing in duplicate, then triple, each demanding payment in a different currency. Merchants screaming that their goods were vanishing. The geometry itself fragmenting. Torvin had two paths forward, both offered by faction brokers. The Counter-Archive wanted him to carry false maps through the Hearthlands, spreading conflicting prophecies to prevent the Ninth Iteration from manifesting. They offered safe passage, coin, and the knowledge that he'd be fighting the cosmic cycle itself. The Dominion auditors wanted him to return with a full ledger of Scar prices and merchant identities. In exchange, they'd protect his family in Keshtir—or at least, they promised they would. Their ledgers contradicted each other now; no promise was certain.

stories.title: The Widow's Second Compass
stories.text: Sorenna had kept the compass her dead husband carved for three winters, watching it spin uselessly in her hands. The needle pointed not north but toward the pre-human boundary where he fell—a wound in the world that refused to close. When the Remaking shattered gold into dust, the boundary began to sing. A stranger arrived at her cottage in the high moors: Kellen, a covenant-keeper from the verdant lands, scarred and quiet. He brought word that the boundary was dying, that the old seals her husband died protecting were unraveling, and that someone needed to walk into that singing dark and renew them. Sorenna built him a second compass—bone and starlight-glass, carved with syllables no kingdom recognized. As she worked, Kellen watched her hands move with the certainty of someone who had loved deeply and lost utterly. "You could come with me," he said. She did. For seven days they walked through mist-thick forests where the air tasted of music older than names.

stories.title: The Glassblower's Defiance
stories.text:
In Keshtir's Lampwick Quarter, where the furnace-hearts still burned with stolen consciousness, Mella refused the auditors' newest commission: a ledger-lens designed to read phantom-documents and expose refugee forgeries. Forty years she had blown glass for the Dominion. Forty years of watching fragments of shattered people melt into weapons and vessels. Mella barred her workshop door with iron salvaged from the Rust Gate itself. "I will not," she said, "burn another person's soul for certainty."

They brought flame-casters. She had expected this. What she had not expected was for the other glass-forgers—Corvin, Telyn, a dozen apprentices—to link arms across the kiln-house threshold. The furnace roared behind them, singing in frequencies that made the auditors' ledgers begin to smoke and curl. Kressa, who for three hundred years had hoarded gold and now hoarded something stranger: mercy. She landed on the Lampwick Quarter's copper-slate roof and spoke in a voice like grinding stone: "The furnace remembers. Glass remembers. I have forgotten how to forget. Will you forget, little people, for coin?"

The auditors fled.

stories.title: The Tiderunner's Bargain
stories.text: Captain Vess had stolen a portion of the Drowned God's peace from the Dominion's harbor vault before the Remaking broke the world. It rested in a sealed flask, luminous and restless, kept in the lowest hold of her tide-runner. The deepkin called it a relic. The Dominion called it theft. Vess called it insurance. When Captain Merenna appeared at the Verdant Strand docks—fractured into three simultaneous selves, each one a captain of a different moment—Vess recognized a woman drowning in choice. The boundary was failing. The Tear of the Drowned God was weakening. The deepkin's covenant, bound for a thousand years on blood-sacrifice, would either renew or break. "Come aboard," Vess said to all three Merennas at once. They sailed northwest, away from charted waters, toward the abyssal boundary where the seal sang its dying song. Vess brought the flask. Merenna brought the weight of three captains' simultaneous oaths. Between them they brought neither certainty nor salvation—only a choice made together, freely, on each tide.

stories.title: The Thief-Queen's Census
stories.text: Kess of the Drowned Hollows had spent five winters as a thief-queen in the shadow-markets, and the Remaking had made her work both easier and impossible: consciousness-fragments were the new currency, the Dominion's vaults were fragmenting, and everyone wanted to know who held what memory of what truth. When Gorven the Conjurer brought her a list—names of imprisoned refugees whose consciousness had been requisitioned into the Quarantine-House beneath Keshtir's administrative heart—Kess made a decision that would have cost her the throne five years prior. She would steal them back. Not alone. She assembled a crew: Marin the Copper-Handed, whose lock-picks sang pre-Remaking frequencies; Davrin the Locksmith, who understood the old phrase "a lock is only as strong as the secret it keeps"; and Thessa, a young caravan scout who could navigate by the echo of her own heartbeat through stone. They descended seven floors beneath Keshtir, past auditor patrols and phantom-ledgers that rewrote themselves in real-time.

stories.title: The Starborn's Wager
stories.text:
Zareth the Astronomer-Prince hung suspended in the cosmic void, his consciousness scattered across the night sky like spilled starlight. For weeks he had maintained the stars' renewal, paying the price in fragments of his own awareness. Then, on the night of the seventh dawn, he simply... stopped. The stars went dark for three heartbeats. Velka the wyvern, who had spent three hundred years watching the sky through the smoke of her own hoarded gold, felt the stars' absence like a wound. She flew to the peak where the cosmic working had begun and found Zareth's echo-self burning at the boundary between worlds, barely coherent. "Why did you release them?" Velka asked. Zareth's scattered mind assembled enough to speak: "Because maintaining them was hoarding too. Because the stars should choose whether to burn, and I was not their keeper—I was their jailer."

Velka landed beside him—a dragon the size of a fortress, her eyes holding the weight of three centuries' worth of questions about what deserved to be kept.
